planting.note: Plant divisions in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant in full sun in average, well-drained soil, giving the big clump room to expand. The very fine silvery-green blades form a graceful fountain topped by coppery plumes in fall. Leave the foliage standing for winter structure and cut it to the ground in early spring.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or plant divisions; it self-sows freely.

directions: Sow seed shallowly or plant divisions in moist, rich soil in sun to partial shade, where it takes more shade than most grasses. The broad bamboo-like leaves are topped by flat, drooping oat-like seed heads that dangle and rustle. The seed heads dry to bronze; cut plants back in late winter and thin volunteer seedlings if it spreads too far.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; grow only the named red form, never seed.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained soil in sun to light shade where backlight sets the blades glowing. The upright green leaves are tipped in deep blood red that spreads down as the season advances. Remove any all-green shoots promptly, since the reverting green form is aggressively invasive; this red selection is grown from division only.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Set the crown level with the surrounding soil in a sunny, sharply drained bed and water until established. This selection is prized for an especially dense, deep-pink autumn haze, so give it open space where light can shine through the plumes. Divide crowded clumps in early spring and shear the old foliage before growth resumes.

planting.note: Plant in warm spring soil in full sun.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew previously and firm the soil gently around the roots. This taller form carries creamy white flowering panicles that glow in late-season light and often open a few weeks after the pink types. Leave the plumes standing for winter interest, then cut back hard before spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean soil.

directions: Set the clump in a sunny, well-drained spot with room to reach its full size. Selected for abundant golden-tinted fall plumes over blue-green foliage, it makes a bold specimen or screen. Water through the first season, then rely on rainfall; cut old growth back before new blades emerge.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; hardier than pink muhly.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a sunny, sharply drained bed. This selection is valued for cold hardiness and a rosy-red autumn cloud of bloom over tidy, fine-textured foliage. Give it lean conditions for the best form and cut back the clump in late winter.

planting.note: Sow or transplant in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Sow seed on warm soil or set plants to form glossy dark-green clumps. Arching plumes emerge purplish then age to soft tan, adding a long season of movement. A southwestern native, it takes heat and drought once established and benefits from a spring cutback.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring with room for a robust clump.

directions: Give this vigorous selection open space in full sun and well-drained soil. It forms a larger, more uniform clump than the wild species, with abundant arching plumes that shift from rosy purple to buff. Water during establishment, then treat as drought tolerant and shear back in late winter.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Barely cover seed on warm, well-drained soil or set out plants in a sunny bed. Slender flowering stems carry a haze of deep purple that catches backlight beautifully in autumn. It is drought tolerant and low maintenance; cut the clump to a few inches before new growth begins.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a compact, hardy selection.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained spot. This lower, tidier form flowers with dark purple plumes over fine foliage and shows better cold tolerance than the species. Give it lean ground and full sun, and shear back once a year in late winter.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a hot, dry, sunny location.

directions: Sow seed on warm, sharply drained soil or set out small plants a foot or two apart. Fine green blades form a neat fountain topped by narrow, silvery-tan flowering spikes in fall. This rugged native of dry rocky slopes needs almost no water once established and stays tidy with a spring trim.

planting.note: Plant once nights warm; it appreciates heat.

directions: Set plants in warm, well-drained soil where the soft blue-gray, felted foliage can catch the light. Airy tan flower plumes rise above the mound in late summer and fall. Prized for its foliage color, it wants full sun, sharp drainage, and minimal water; refresh with a spring cutback.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, well-drained coastal soil.

directions: Sow seed on warm sandy soil or set out plants where they get full sun and good air movement. This coastal southeastern grass, long woven into sweetgrass baskets, throws a stunning pink to purple flower cloud in fall. It tolerates salt spray and sandy ground; leave the plumes for winter and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring; adapted to high, dry ground.

directions: Barely cover seed on warm, well-drained soil in an open, sunny site. This small clumping native of the western uplands forms a low tuft topped by dense, narrow gray-green flower spikes in summer. It is cold hardy and drought tolerant, making a fine choice for meadows and rock gardens.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring; tolerates moist to average soils.

directions: Sow seed on warm soil in a spot with average to moist ground and some afternoon relief. This cold-hardy eastern and midwestern native spreads by wiry, branching stems and produces slender, purplish-tan flower panicles in late summer. It suits naturalistic and rain-garden plantings and can be cut back in late winter.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant to acidic, well-drained ground in spring.

directions: Sow on the surface of a lean, acidic mix and keep lightly moist until sprouts appear. This fine-textured grass tolerates dry shade and poor sandy soils better than most, making it useful under trees. Delicate wiry stems carry shimmering bronze-purple flower heads in early summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring or early fall in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and water well while roots establish. This compact selection forms a tidy green mound topped by clouds of flowers that open green and age to warm gold. Leave the airy seed heads standing for autumn and winter interest, then shear back in late winter.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring or fall in evenly moist ground.

directions: Set divisions into rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep watered through the first season. The tall flowering panicles emerge greenish and mature to a soft bronze-yellow, hovering above a neat evergreen tussock. Cut the clump back to a few inches before growth resumes in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist soil.

directions: Space divisions to give the flower cloud room to show and mulch to hold soil moisture. Slender stems lift a shimmering veil of flowers that ripen to bright golden-yellow in mid to late summer. It performs best where summers stay cool and roots never dry out completely.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant at the original crown depth and keep the soil steadily moist. As the name suggests, this selection flowers later than most, extending the airy display into late summer. Leave the tawny panicles up over winter for structure and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil with afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Give this small, brightly variegated grass steady moisture and shelter from harsh afternoon sun. The cream-and-green striped blades flush pink in cool weather and it rarely flowers, valued instead for its low, colorful mound. Trim out any tired foliage and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep the variegation vigorous.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring or fall in fertile, moist ground.

directions: Plant this robust selection in rich, evenly moist soil and give it plenty of room. It is one of the taller and more vigorous forms, sending flowering stems well above the dense green tussock. The panicles open green and mature to golden-tan, standing handsomely into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set out divisions at their former depth and keep them watered while establishing. Arching stems carry a shower of flowers that turn rich golden-yellow by midsummer over a compact green mound. Leave the seed heads for late-season sparkle, then cut back before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in cool, moist soil.

directions: Give this curious form the same cool, moisture-retentive conditions the species enjoys. Instead of seeds, the flower heads produce tiny plantlets that weigh the stems down in graceful arches. The little plantlets can be pinned into moist soil to root and start new clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil; suits containers and edges.

directions: Set this dwarf selection near the front of a border or in a pot where its scale reads well. It makes a compact green mound topped by fine golden flower sprays in summer. Keep the roots cool and moist, and shear old growth back before new blades emerge in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in acidic, well-drained soil with some shade.

directions: Site this bright selection in cool part shade where its yellow-gold blades glow against darker foliage. It prefers lean, acidic, well-drained soil and dislikes soggy winter ground. Wiry stems lift airy bronze-tinged flowers above the golden mound in early summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in fertile, evenly moist soil.

directions: Plant this selection in rich, moisture-holding soil and water through dry spells. Fine flowering panicles catch dew and morning light above a rounded green clump. Let the fading heads stand for autumn interest before cutting the clump back in late winter.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in spring or lift and divide clumps as they break dormancy.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a moist, humus-rich mix and keep it damp and cool until germination. Site it in full sun with steady moisture so the tall flowering stems reach full height. Slender see-through panicles sway on wiry stems from midsummer, and the whole plant turns amber before winter.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before in moist, slightly acidic soil. This is a stiff, narrowly upright form that holds its shape without staking, so give it steady moisture and full sun. Dark, tight flower spikes stand strictly vertical from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into consistently moist ground.

directions: Settle divisions into rich, moisture-retentive soil and water well until established. This upright selection sends slim golden flower stems well above the leaf mound and needs little support. Cut the clump to the ground in late winter to make way for fresh spring growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the dark stems catch backlight.

directions: Plant into moist, acidic soil and keep it evenly watered through the growing season. Prized for near-black flower stems and dark seed heads, it looks best sited where morning or evening light shines through the panicles. Foliage yellows in autumn, then the whole plant should be trimmed to the crown in late winter.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring for the brightest new variegation.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in full sun or light shade. The leaves are boldly striped cream and green, forming a tidy mound topped by tan flower stems in late summer. Water in dry spells to keep the foliage fresh, and cut old growth away before spring.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring with room for the arching form to spread.

directions: Plant in evenly moist, acidic soil and keep watered until well rooted. The flowering stems arch outward in a graceful fountain rather than standing bolt upright, so give it space at the front of a border. Golden autumn color follows the airy summer bloom.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into moist ground.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth in rich, moisture-retentive soil. This selection forms a loose, arching clump with slender flower stems that persist well into fall. Keep the roots cool and moist, and cut the plant back to the base each late winter.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring for an upright late-summer display.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil and water regularly during establishment. Upright leaf tufts give rise to tall, slim flowering stems that add vertical accent to the border. Leave the dried stems for winter structure and cut down before growth resumes.

planting.note: Divide in spring and replant into moist, acidic soil.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, evenly moist soil in light shade or full sun. The leaves carry crisp cream-and-green variegation and form a neat mound below tan flower stems. Keep watered in summer to prevent leaf scorch, and remove spent growth before spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where you can see through the airy stems.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, moisture-retentive soil and water well until rooted. The tall flowering stems carry such fine, widely spaced seeds that you can see straight through the veil they form. Site it where light and movement show off the transparent effect, and cut to the base in late winter.

planting.note: Give this tall selection room and set divisions out in spring.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist soil in full sun for the greatest height. One of the tallest moor grasses, it launches slim golden flower stems well above the low foliage mound. The stems stand through autumn and turn deep amber before you cut them down in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in an open spot that catches a breeze.

directions: Set divisions into deep, moist soil and keep watered through the first summer. The tall, loosely held flower stems sway and dance in the slightest wind, giving the plant its name. Enjoy the golden autumn color, then cut the whole clump to the ground before spring.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions at their previous depth and water in thoroughly. This vigorous, upright selection carries tall arching flower stems above a compact base of foliage. Give it steady moisture and full sun, and cut back the dried stems each late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water until established. The gracefully arching flower stems rise from a neat clump and glow golden in fall. Keep roots cool and moist through summer, then remove the old growth before new shoots appear.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring with space for the fountain shape.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, moist soil and water well during establishment. The flowering stems arch outward like a fountain, spreading wider than the upright forms. Give it room at the middle of a border, and cut the plant to the base in late winter.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant at the original depth in rich, moisture-retentive ground and water in well. This tall, upright selection holds its flowering stems high above a modest foliage clump. It colors warm gold in autumn before the stems are cut down in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where a strong vertical accent is wanted.

directions: Set divisions into deep, moist soil and keep watered until rooted. As the name suggests, this form holds its tall flower stems in a tight, narrow column rather than arching. It suits a vertical accent in the border and should be cut to the crown before spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring into moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, moisture-retentive soil and water regularly while establishing. This tall selection produces upright flowering stems that tower over the base foliage. Provide steady moisture and full sun, and remove the dried stems each late winter.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into moist ground.

directions: Plant divisions at their previous depth in rich, moist soil and water in well. The clump sends up tall, airy flowering stems that catch light and movement above the low leaves. Let the golden autumn foliage stand for interest, then cut the plant back before spring.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after frost danger passes in spring.

directions: Surface sow the tiny seed and firm it into warm soil, misting until germination. This selected strain forms an especially soft, uniform fountain of thread-fine leaves that shimmer in the breeze. Trim spent silky plumes in late summer to limit self-seeding, and comb out or shear the tired foliage before spring growth resumes.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring as growth begins.

directions: Plant this selection with its crown at soil level in lean, freely draining ground and full sun. Prized for taller, more freely produced golden flowering stems, it holds an evergreen basal tuft in mild climates. Divide congested clumps in spring, and shear last year's stalks before new leaves emerge; the shimmering plumes appear from early summer.

planting.note: Formerly sold as Stipa arundinacea; start indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Sow seed under cover in spring and grow on before planting into moist but well-drained soil in sun or light shade. The arching evergreen foliage flushes with olive, orange, and bronze tints, especially in cooler weather, and airy flowering sprays drape over the clump in summer. Comb out dead blades by hand rather than shearing, and let it self-sow gently to replace short-lived clumps.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a period of cold before sowing can improve germination.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and lightly moist to sprout. Give this grass gritty, alkaline, well-drained soil and full sun, avoiding rich or wet ground. In early summer it produces long, silky, feather-like awns that ripple dramatically in the wind; cut the clump back in late winter and expect first flowering in the second year.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring and transplant once settled weather arrives.

directions: Cover the seed thinly and grow on in a warm, bright spot before planting into sharply drained, sunny ground. This tough steppe grass forms a tidy tuft topped in late summer by hair-fine, twisting awns that turn straw-gold. Leave the airy heads to shimmer into autumn, then tidy the clump in early spring.

planting.note: Sow in spring; needs very free drainage to thrive.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and grow young plants on before setting them into gritty, lean, sun-baked ground. In early summer it unfurls extraordinarily long, curling silver awns that twist and glint like spun thread. Protect from winter wet, cut back old growth in late winter, and let it seed around lightly to sustain the planting.

planting.note: Long grown as Stipa calamagrostis; start indoors or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Cover seed lightly to germinate, or plant divisions in spring into well-drained, sunny soil. This dependable clumping grass throws up dense, arching, feathery flower spikes that open silvery and age to soft buff. The plumes hold beautifully for months and dry well for arrangements; cut the whole clump to the ground in late winter.

planting.note: Sow in spring into gritty mix; provide excellent drainage outdoors.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm to sprout, then grow on before planting into a hot, sharply drained, sunny bed. Among the showiest feather grasses, it produces very long, silky, plume-like awns in early summer that flutter with every breeze. Avoid soggy winter soil, cut back spent growth in late winter, and look for reliable flowering from the second year.

planting.note: Sometimes listed as Stipa ichu; start indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface sow and keep warm and lightly moist, then grow young plants on before transplanting into well-drained, sunny soil. This tender Andean grass forms a neat tuft crowned with fluffy, brilliant white, feathery plumes that glow when backlit. In colder zones treat it as a container or annual accent and overwinter under cover; shear tired growth in early spring.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring; thrives on lean, dry, alkaline soils.

directions: Cover the seed thinly and germinate in warmth before planting into gritty, well-drained ground in full sun. This compact steppe grass makes a fine, blue-green tuft topped by delicate silvery awns in early summer. It is exceptionally drought tolerant once established; cut back old foliage in late winter to keep the clump neat.

planting.note: Long known as Stipa spartea; sow the sharp-pointed seed in spring or fall.

directions: Sow the large, sharply awned seed about a quarter inch deep into a prepared prairie bed; a cold moist period improves sprouting. This North American native forms a bunchgrass whose long, spiraling awns twist with humidity and drill seed into the ground. Give it full sun and lean soil, and cut back the previous year's growth before new spring shoots emerge.

planting.note: Formerly Stipa comata; sow in spring or dormant-sow in fall.

directions: Plant the slender seed shallowly into dry, well-drained ground; fall dormant-sowing supplies the cold it appreciates. This Western native cool-season bunchgrass bears fine, thread-like awns that shimmer above the foliage in late spring and early summer. Once established it is extremely drought hardy; leave it largely undisturbed and trim old growth as new leaves appear.

planting.note: Also listed as Stipa viridula; sow spring or fall into prairie soil.

directions: Sow shallowly into a firm, weed-free bed in spring or dormant-seed in late fall. This cool-season prairie bunchgrass greens up early and produces narrow, nodding seed heads with short awns by early summer. It tolerates a range of soils and cold winters; cut back the previous season's stems in early spring to make way for fresh growth.

planting.note: Also Nassella lepida; sow in fall for cool-season establishment.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly onto prepared ground in autumn and keep lightly moist until seedlings root in. This slender California native bunchgrass tolerates part shade under oaks and produces delicate, awned flowering panicles in spring. It goes dormant in summer drought; give it minimal water and cut back tired growth before the fall rains return.

planting.note: Also Nassella cernua; autumn sowing follows its natural rhythm.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in fall so plants establish during the moist cool months. This California grassland native forms a fine-textured bunch and bears gracefully nodding, long-awned flower heads in spring. Deep-rooted and drought adapted, it goes summer-dormant; cut back the dormant clump and let winter rains restart growth.

planting.note: Formerly Stipa robusta; sow in spring into dry, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly in spring into lean, freely draining ground in full sun. This robust Southwestern bunchgrass forms a tall, upright clump topped with narrow, feathery flowering panicles in summer. It is highly drought tolerant once rooted; note that the foliage contains alkaloids and is best kept away from grazing livestock, and cut old stems back in late winter.

planting.note: Long grown as Stipa splendens; start indoors and plant into a large open site.

directions: Cover seed lightly to germinate, then grow on before planting into deep, well-drained soil with plenty of room. This large steppe grass builds a dense fountain of tough leaves and sends up towering, airy, silvery flowering plumes in mid to late summer. Tolerant of drought, cold, and even saline soils, it is best cut back hard once a year in late winter.

planting.note: Start indoors in spring; requires very sharp drainage in the garden.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep warm to sprout, growing young plants on before transplanting to a hot, gritty, sunny spot. This Central Asian species produces long, silky, feathered awns in early summer that ripple beautifully in wind. Guard against winter wet, shear off spent growth in late winter, and expect strong flowering from the second season.

planting.note: Set out potted divisions after frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew in the pot and water in well while roots settle. This selection carries larger chartreuse flags that mature to blond and hold upright through winter for lasting interest. Cut the clump back to a few inches in early spring before new green growth pushes.

planting.note: Direct sow in late spring after soil warms past 70 degrees.

directions: Rake seed lightly into a firm bed and water often enough to keep the surface damp until the low turf fills in. This selection is prized as a denser, shorter, cold-hardy lawn grass for the northern plains. Left unmown it produces small flag-like flowers held close over the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Sow in late spring once the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Broadcast seed onto firmed soil, cover shallowly, and keep moist through establishment before easing back on water. This seeded type suits low-maintenance lawns and prairie plantings across the central plains. It greens up in late spring and cures to a soft tan after autumn frost.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm late-spring soil for best emergence.

directions: Sow shallowly into a firm seedbed and water lightly and often until the turf establishes. This southern-adapted seeded selection makes a fine-textured, drought-tough lawn. Skip mowing in early summer to enjoy the delicate flag flowers held just above the blades.

planting.note: Sow after frost when soil has warmed for reliable germination.

directions: Rake seed into firmed ground no deeper than a quarter inch and keep the bed damp until seedlings take hold. This long-established seeded selection is grown for both durable turf and rangeland cover. It forms a resilient sod that shrugs off heat and drought once rooted.

planting.note: Set plugs into warm soil in late spring so runners can spread all season.

directions: Plant vegetative plugs at the recommended spacing into loosened soil and water regularly until the runners connect. This dense, fine-textured selection is established from plugs rather than seed and knits into a soft lawn. Once filled in it needs little mowing and very little supplemental water.

planting.note: Plant plugs in late spring to give runners a full warm season to fill in.

directions: Set vegetative plugs into prepared soil and keep them watered until the stolons weave together into turf. This cold-hardy vegetative selection makes a dense, deep blue-green lawn with few seed heads. After establishment it tolerates drought and needs only occasional mowing.

planting.note: Plug in late spring so the grass can knit before summer heat peaks.

directions: Install plugs at even spacing and water in well, keeping the soil moist while runners spread. This vegetative selection greens up early and holds a rich color with a soft, fine texture. It forms a low, dense lawn that stays presentable with minimal mowing.

planting.note: Set plugs in late spring for a full season of stolon spread.

directions: Plant plugs into loosened, weed-free soil and water consistently during the first weeks. This established vegetative selection spreads vigorously to form a soft, low-growing turf. Once knit together it thrives on little water and infrequent mowing.

planting.note: Plant plugs after frost from spring into early summer in warm regions.

directions: Set plugs into prepared soil and keep them moist until the runners join into turf. Bred for warm, dry western climates, this selection stays greener and finer-textured than seeded types. After it fills in, water deeply but infrequently and mow only as needed.

planting.note: Plug in late spring or early summer once soil is warm.

directions: Plant vegetative plugs at recommended spacing and irrigate until the stolons close the gaps. This female selection produces very few seed heads for a clean, uniform lawn. It handles heat and drought well and needs only light mowing to look tidy.

planting.note: Set plugs after frost in warm-climate spring or early summer.

directions: Plant plugs into cleared soil and keep evenly moist while the runners spread out. This dwarf vegetative selection stays especially low and dense, cutting down on mowing. Once established it forms a soft, drought-tolerant carpet in hot regions.

planting.note: Plug in late spring so the grass establishes before peak heat.

directions: Install plugs at even spacing and water regularly until the turf fills in. As the name suggests, this vegetative selection forms an especially tight, uniform sod. It makes a low-input lawn that resists weeds once fully knit together.

planting.note: Sow into warm spring soil in a dry, sunny spot.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and rake in lightly, watering just enough to start the seedlings. This small clumping grama is an early-season bloomer well suited to hot, dry native gardens. Slender seed spikes cluster near the stem tips and cure to straw as summer advances.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm spring soil on lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Sow shallowly into firmed soil and keep barely moist until the delicate tufts establish. This dainty native grama tolerates rocky, dry sites and asks for little care. Its thread-fine flower spikes take on a reddish tint in early summer before fading to tan.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew before and water well while they settle in, since this white-striped selection does not come true from seed. Grow in light shade to keep the creamy variegation crisp and prevent leaf scorch. Airy seed heads appear in late summer above the striped foliage and dry to warm tan for fall interest.

planting.note: Sow shallowly after the soil has warmed in spring.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the surface damp until sprouts appear. This more upright, narrow-leaved species tolerates dry shade once established and forms tidy clumps. Slim spikelets rise on thin stems in late summer and hold into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring once frost has passed, or start indoors after cold stratification.

directions: Sow just below the surface and maintain even moisture during establishment. This woodland species thrives in moist, shaded ground and spreads gently to form colonies. Narrow, stalkless spikelets sit close along the stems and mature to a soft straw color in fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost into a moist, shaded bed.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it consistently damp until it germinates. This southeastern species favors shaded, moist bottomland and forms slender, glossy-leaved clumps. Its small shining spikelets ripen through late summer and fade to tan by frost.

planting.note: Sow on the surface of warm soil in spring; a native of the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Press seed lightly into a sandy, sharply drained bed and keep barely moist until germination. This blue-green clumping grass shrugs off heat, drought, and salt spray once rooted, making it a good coastal choice. Airy silver-white flower panicles form a cloud above the foliage in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew, firming sandy, well-drained soil around the roots and watering in. This selection is grown from divisions rather than seed to keep its uniform blue-green foliage and tidy fountain shape. Diaphanous white-to-pink flower clouds sway on the slightest breeze from summer into autumn, giving the plant its name.

planting.note: Direct-sow into warm sandy soil in spring; a selected release of sand lovegrass.

directions: Broadcast seed on a firm, sandy bed and rake in only lightly, keeping it barely moist until sprouting. This vigorous selection was chosen for reliable stands and good drought hardiness on dry, well-drained sites. Tall airy flower panicles rise well above the leaves in late summer, aging to a soft straw color into fall.

planting.note: Sow into warm spring soil once frosts have ended.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly onto a firm bed and keep just moist until it germinates. A tough South African clumper, it withstands heat and drought and prefers lean, well-drained ground. Gray-green foliage carries fine, dark-tinged flower panicles through summer and holds its form well into winter.

planting.note: Sow into warm spring soil after frost; a warm-climate African species.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm, well-drained soil and keep lightly moist until seedlings establish. It is prized for its unusual flattened, heart-shaped flower spikelets that stand out among grasses. The distinctive reddish-brown seed heads appear in summer and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on firmed soil and keep just moist until germination. This modest tufted grass tolerates poor, dry ground and full sun with little fuss. It is named for its small heart-shaped spikelets that dangle from airy panicles through the warm months.

planting.note: Direct-sow after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Scatter seed on a firm surface and rake in very lightly, keeping the bed moist until seedlings emerge. This fast, warm-season annual grass fills in quickly and reseeds readily on open sunny ground. Loose, spreading flower panicles rise above the foliage in late summer and fade to tan as the seed ripens.

planting.note: Sow into warm spring soil; a drought-hardy African species that can spread aggressively in dry climates.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly onto firm, well-drained soil and keep just moist through germination. It is exceptionally drought tolerant and roots from trailing stems, so give it room and be aware it can self-sow freely in arid regions. Fine gray-green foliage carries slender flowering panicles through the warm season.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring after the last hard frost so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown level to the soil surface and water in well until new growth resumes. This selection stays more compact and upright than the wild species, making it useful in tighter borders and mass plantings. Slender pink-tinged flower stalks appear in late summer and the mound colors up orange to burgundy before fading to bronze for winter.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once soil is workable and frost danger has passed.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in lean, sharply drained soil and keep weeds down while the slow-establishing clump fills in. This northern seed strain is vigorous and cold-hardy, forming a dense fountain of hair-fine leaves. Fragrant open panicles float above the foliage in late summer, followed by warm amber and orange autumn tones.

planting.note: Sow or plant out in spring after frost as soil warms for best establishment.

directions: Sow onto a warm, firmed bed and thin seedlings to give this large grass room to develop. It builds a bold, arching clump of gray-green foliage that works as a specimen or informal screen and tolerates heat, drought, and periodic flooding. Tall feathery panicles rise well above the leaves in late summer, drying to straw color for winter structure.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost so the large clump settles before summer.

directions: Set plants with the crown at soil level and space them widely to allow for the substantial mature size. This tall, robust selection is prized as a fast living windbreak or vertical accent in dry gardens. Upright plumes tower over the blue-green blades in late summer and hold as bleached tan wands through the cold months.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost onto warm, sandy soil.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on a firm, warm seedbed and barely cover it, as this pioneer grass favors loose sandy ground and dry conditions. It makes a slender tuft that spreads readily and stabilizes bare or eroding soil. A narrow, spike-like panicle emerges partly sheathed in late summer and opens into an airy, purplish cloud as it matures.

planting.note: Sow or plant out in spring once frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Sow shallowly on a firmed bed and keep lightly moist until the wiry seedlings establish. This tough native forms an upright clump that tolerates poor, dry soils and full exposure. Slender contracted panicles rise in late summer and cure to a soft tan, adding fine vertical texture to naturalistic plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after the last frost as soils warm.

directions: Press seed into a warm, firm seedbed and water lightly until the fine-textured tuft is established. It handles dry, rocky, or sandy ground and needs little care once settled. Narrow flowering spikes stay partly hidden within the upper leaf sheaths in late summer, giving the clump a quiet, delicate look before turning tawny for winter.

planting.note: Sow in spring after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Scatter seed on a warm, firmed bed and cover lightly, giving this warm-climate grass full sun and free-draining ground. It builds a tufted clump of soft green foliage that greens up quickly with summer rains and tolerates grazing and drought. Loose, fringed panicles rise on fine stems through the warm season and dry to a pale golden veil.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring once the ground has warmed and settled.

directions: Plant crowns at the same depth they grew in the pot, spacing them for a solid mat over a couple of seasons. Give lean, well-drained soil and only occasional water once established, as this grass resents wet feet. Comb out old foliage in late winter and watch for the silvery flower spikes that rise above the chartreuse mound from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall into gritty, well-drained ground.

directions: Tuck small divisions into a sharply drained, even limy soil and firm them in gently. This low tussock shows green above and steely blue-white beneath, so plant it where the two-toned leaves catch the light. Short black-and-white flower heads appear in early spring; shear tired foliage in late winter to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant at soil level in average, well-drained ground and mulch lightly the first season. The foliage is blue-green on top with a paler reverse and holds well into winter in milder zones. Look for the dark, near-black flower spikes in early to mid spring, one of the earliest of the moor grasses to bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and gritty, alkaline-leaning soil.

directions: Set plants at their original depth and give them the leanest, best-drained spot you have. The broad silvery-gray blades form a striking pale mound that reads almost white in strong sun. Slender flower stalks carry pale spikes above the foliage in mid to late spring; cut back old growth before new leaves emerge.

planting.note: Increase this selection by division in spring, as it is not grown true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil and space them to knit into a low, uniform groundcover. This vigorous hybrid blends the two-toned green-and-blue foliage of its parents and stays dense and tidy. Thin flower spikes rise over the clump through the warmer months; shear the whole planting once in late winter to renew it.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil surface in a gritty, well-drained bed and water sparingly once rooted. The broad blue-green leaves are wider than most moor grasses and form a bold, rounded tuft. Pale flower spikes appear in spring; groom out spent foliage in late winter to keep the mound fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it appreciates warmth and excellent drainage.

directions: Plant into lean, fast-draining soil in full sun and avoid overwatering. The narrow green foliage sends up tall, slender stems topped with silvery flower spikes that shimmer in summer light. Leave the seed heads for late-season interest and cut the clump back in late winter before new growth begins.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once the soil has warmed and hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew previously, keeping the crown level with the soil surface, and water in well to settle the roots. Grow in full sun for the deepest red blade color, as too much shade fades the tips toward green. The upright blades emerge green at the base and turn wine-red from the tips down, with the richest coloring developing through summer and into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost when the soil is workable and warming.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level and firm the soil gently around the roots before watering. Give this clump a spot with full sun and evenly moist, well-drained soil to bring out its vivid blood-red blade tips. The foliage glows brightest when backlit by low sun, deepening from summer through autumn before dying back for winter.

planting.note: Establish divisions or seedlings in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained soil in full sun, spacing clumps to allow the tall stems room to arch. This drought-tolerant native needs little supplemental water once established and tolerates dry, sandy ground. Look for silvery-white feathery plumes rising above the foliage in late summer, catching light like satin ribbons.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring once hard frosts have passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before in constantly moist to boggy ground, or grow in a pot standing in a saucer of water. Cut old foliage back to the base in late winter to make room for fresh green growth. Small brown flower clusters appear along the stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost in wet soil or a container that never dries out.

directions: Set this cultivar in permanently moist soil or at a pond margin, keeping the crown at soil level. Remove any straight upright shoots that revert from the coiled form and trim ratty stems in early spring. Grown for its curling corkscrew stems rather than its small summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into wet ground or a water-holding container.

directions: Grow in boggy soil or a pot set in water, keeping roots wet through the season. Pull out any reverting straight stems to preserve the tighter, more strongly spiraled habit. Enjoy the twisting stems all season and cut back tired growth in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet soil, at a pond edge, or in a container.

directions: This dwarf rush wants steady moisture, so plant it in a bog, a boggy border, or a pot standing in water. Keep the crown at soil level and comb out dead stems in early spring. Its low fountain of tight green corkscrews is the main show; flowers are inconspicuous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in reliably wet soil or a bog container.

directions: Set in constantly moist soil or a submerged pot, keeping the base wet. Grow in full sun to bring out the best yellow lengthwise banding on the upright stems. Remove any all-green reverting shoots and cut back in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet ground or a large water-holding pot.

directions: This vigorous, larger-leaved corkscrew form needs a bog, pond margin, or pot standing in water. Keep the crown at soil level and remove straight reverting stems as they appear. Cut back old growth in early spring for a flush of thick spiraling stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring into moist or wet soil once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant in wet or seasonally flooded ground, keeping the crown level with the soil. This tougher, blue-green rush tolerates drier spells than soft rush but is happiest at pond edges. Small brownish flower clusters form on the stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet soil, a bog, or a pot standing in water.

directions: Set this stiffly upright, blue-gray rush in constantly moist soil or at a water feature. Keep it wet and in full sun for the most vertical, arrow-straight stems. Shear old foliage to the base in late winter before new growth starts.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet soil or a container held in water.

directions: Grow this loosely curling, silvery-green rush in bog soil or a pot set in a saucer of water. Keep it moist and remove any straight reverting stems to keep the twisted look. Its tangled spiral stems are the feature; flowers are minor.

planting.note: Plant in spring; established plants tolerate seasonal drought better than most rushes.

directions: Plant this adaptable western rush in average to wet soil, keeping the crown at grade. Once established it accepts periods of dryness, making it useful in rain gardens and low-water plantings. Fine blue-gray stems form an upright clump topped by small summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in average to wet soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Set this selection out in spring in moist but well-drained to wet soil. Prized for its strong steel-blue color, it holds up in rain gardens and takes some drought once rooted. Comb out dead stems in late winter to refresh the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in average to wet soil in full sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this taller, silvery-gray rush at the same depth it grew in the pot in moist to wet ground. It works in bog gardens yet tolerates dry spells once established. Cut back to a few inches in late winter to keep the clump tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet soil, at pond edges, or in shallow standing water.

directions: Set this flat, iris-like rush in wet soil or shallow water at a pond margin. It spreads by rhizomes to form a colony, so give it room or contain it. Distinctive round, dark brown flower heads appear on the stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil; tolerates some salinity and poor drainage.

directions: Plant this rhizomatous rush in wet, even brackish soil where it will slowly spread into a stand. Keep the crown at grade and allow room for its running roots. Stiff dark green stems bear small flower clusters near the tips in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil; tolerates coastal salt and heat.

directions: Give this large, salt-tolerant rush room in wet or coastal soil, and site it away from paths since the leaf tips are genuinely sharp. It forms a big dense clump over time. Rounded reddish-brown seed heads follow the summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet soil or at a pond edge.

directions: Plant in permanently moist to wet soil, keeping the crown level with the surface. This rush is named for its tight, knot-like flower clusters bunched low on the stems. Cut back tired growth in late winter to renew the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet ground or shallow water at a pond margin.

directions: Set in wet mud or shallow standing water where its jointed, hollow stems can spread. Keep it consistently wet through the growing season. Loose branched heads of small dark flowers open in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil; spreads by tuber-bearing rhizomes.

directions: Plant this native rush in wet soil or a rain garden where it can colonize by underground runners. Give it room or a barrier to limit spread. Spiky, spherical flower and seed heads sit atop the upright stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet, acidic soil or at a pond edge.

directions: Plant this clumping native in consistently wet, preferably acidic soil at a wetland margin. Keep it moist and it will form a tidy tussock. Branched heads of bristly, straw-brown seed clusters top the stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich shade or part sun.

directions: Plant this golden-leaved selection where it gets some light to intensify the color, in leafy soil that stays evenly moist. The foliage glows chartreuse to yellow-green and brightens most in winter and early spring. Small brown flower heads appear over the leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Use this cream-edged woodrush as an evergreen groundcover in shade, planting into leaf-rich soil. Each broad glossy leaf carries a fine white margin that lights up dim corners. Trim any winter-scorched leaves in early spring before the flower sprays open.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich shade to part shade.

directions: Plant this compact, fresh-green woodrush selection as ground cover in leafy shade with steady moisture. It forms neat, low evergreen rosettes that spread slowly to fill in. Soft brown flower clusters rise on thin stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich part shade.

directions: Site this bright yellow-leaved woodrush in part shade with even moisture to keep the color vivid without scorching. It makes a glowing evergreen carpet under shrubs and trees. Fine brown flower heads float above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant this woodrush in cool shade with reliable moisture, where its new spring growth emerges creamy-white before greening. Use it to lighten shady borders and woodland edges. Comb out old foliage in early spring so the fresh pale shoots stand out.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich soil in part shade to shade.

directions: Plant this clumping woodrush in leafy, moisture-retentive soil in dappled shade. Its narrow leaves are edged with fine white hairs that catch the light. Clusters of small white flowers open at the stem tips in early summer, giving the plant its name.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich part shade.

directions: Set this selection in dappled shade with even moisture where its bright white flower heads show best. It forms a tidy clump of hairy-edged green leaves. Numerous snow-white flower clusters appear in early summer above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil in shade.

directions: Plant this low evergreen woodrush as a fine-textured groundcover under trees in leafy soil. Soft white hairs fringe the flat green leaves. Loose sprays of small brown flowers open early in spring on wiry stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average, moist soil in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant this low, mat-forming woodrush in ordinary grassland-type soil, where it tolerates more sun than the woodland species. It is often woven into wildflower lawns and meadows. Nodding clusters of chestnut-brown flowers with yellow anthers open very early in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich soil in part shade to shade.

directions: Plant this loosely tufted woodrush in leafy shade with steady moisture, where it will gently self-sow. Its grassy clumps suit naturalistic woodland plantings. Airy heads of small whitish flowers appear on branching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich part shade.

directions: Plant this creamy-variegated woodrush in dappled shade with even moisture to protect the pale foliage from scorch. New growth emerges brightest and softens as it matures. Comb out old leaves in early spring and enjoy the fresh variegated flush.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring at the water's edge as growth resumes.

directions: Plant divisions in saturated soil or shallow standing water with the rhizome barely covered. The upright blades are boldly striped cream and green, brightest in full sun, and need continuously wet feet to look their best. Cut back tattered foliage in late winter and lift and split clumps every three or four years to keep the variegation vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring along pond and stream margins.

directions: Set rhizome pieces just under the mud in a bog garden or shallow pond shelf where the soil stays wet year-round. This cold-hardy native forms grassy colonies and tolerates seasonal flooding once established. Grow it in full sun to light shade and divide congested stands in spring; slim greenish flower spikes emerge from the leaf edges in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in reliably moist ground or a container pond.

directions: Tuck small divisions into constantly moist soil at a pond edge or in a shady border, keeping the crown at the surface. This low, fine-textured tuft of dark green foliage stays semi-evergreen in mild winters and spreads slowly into a neat mat. Give it partial shade in hot regions and never let the roots dry out; simply shear off winter-worn leaves in early spring.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in damp soil or shallow water.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently wet, humus-rich soil, spacing them close for a flowing carpet. The blades are striped bright gold and green, glowing best with a few hours of sun but scorching in dry sites, so keep the roots wet. This popular selection stays low and tidy; comb out old foliage in late winter and split clumps every few years to maintain color.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in wet ground or a bog container.

directions: Plant divisions with the rhizome at the mud line in a moist border, streamside, or shallow water garden. The narrow leaves carry crisp cream-and-green stripes that light up shady, damp corners. Keep the soil saturated, give partial shade where summers are hot, and refresh clumps by dividing them in spring.

planting.note: Plant tiny divisions in spring in moist soil or at a pond edge.

directions: Nestle the small divisions into wet, rich soil, spacing them a few inches apart for a low golden cushion. This miniature form makes a fine edging, trough, or fairy-garden plant and works between stepping stones that stay damp. Keep it moist at all times and in partial shade in hot climates; trim any winter-burned tips in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet soil or submerged in shallow water.

directions: Set the compact green divisions into saturated soil or shallow standing water, where this tiny selection thrives even fully submerged in aquariums and pond margins. It forms a dense, grassy tuft only a few inches tall, ideal for the front of a bog planting. Keep it constantly wet and in bright shade to gentle sun, dividing when the clump crowds its space.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in moist, rich soil.

directions: Plant divisions in continuously moist ground or shallow water with the crown at the surface. The foliage is a soft, near-solid yellow-gold, brighter than many green-striped forms and luminous in a shady, damp spot. Protect the roots from ever drying out and give partial shade in hot summers; tidy old leaves in late winter and split clumps to keep them dense.

planting.note: Plant small divisions in spring in wet, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant the compact divisions in reliably moist soil at a shady pond margin or in a bog container. Its fine leaves are heavily marked with crisp white striping, giving a cool, frosted look to damp shade. Keep the soil saturated and out of harsh midday sun to prevent scorch, and lift and divide the low clumps in spring to renew them.

planting.note: Set out small divisions in spring once the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant tiny tufts a few inches apart at the same depth they grew, tucking the crown level with the soil surface. It spreads slowly by stolons to knit into a fine turf-like mat that tolerates light foot traffic. Small lilac flowers hide among the blades in summer, followed by occasional blue berries.

planting.note: Lift and split established clumps in spring for new plantings.

directions: Plant the dark green arching tufts in moist, well-drained ground with the crown at soil level. It slowly forms colonies by rhizome and needs only occasional grooming to remove tired leaves. Nodding lilac flowers appear in summer, followed by blue-black berries in fall.

planting.note: Plant small plugs closely in spring for a quick carpet.

directions: Tuck the miniature dark green tufts a few inches apart into loose soil, keeping crowns just at the surface. This very compact selection makes a dense low mat prized for pathways and between stepping stones. It rarely needs mowing and shows tiny lilac summer flowers hidden in the blades.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in bright shade.

directions: Plant the cream-and-green striped clumps in fertile, moist soil where the bright variegation lights up a shady bed. Keep it out of harsh afternoon sun to avoid leaf scorch and water during dry spells. White summer flowers rise on short stems above the arching foliage.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant the white-striped grassy clumps in moist, rich soil to brighten shaded borders and container edges. Give it part shade and consistent water, trimming winter-worn blades in early spring. Small lavender flowers appear among the foliage during summer.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring or early fall in almost any soil.

directions: Set the strappy dark green clumps at soil level and firm them into well-drained ground; they tolerate sun or shade once established. Shear the whole planting to a few inches in late winter to refresh the foliage before new growth. Upright spikes of violet flowers rise above the leaves in late summer, followed by black berries.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring or fall for edging and mass plantings.

directions: Space the vigorous dark green clumps evenly along a border or path and firm into any decent soil. Cut old foliage back hard in late winter so fresh blades emerge clean in spring. Dense spikes of blue-violet flowers stand above the leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for the best white bloom color.

directions: Plant the deep green clumps in a shaded, moist bed where the pure white flowers show best out of strong sun. Keep soil evenly moist and shear old foliage in late winter to renew the planting. Upright spikes of white blooms appear above the leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring or early fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Firm the dark green grassy clumps into well-drained soil along edges or in drifts. This selection is grown for its rich deep-purple flower spikes, so give it enough light to bloom well. Cut back old leaves in late winter before new growth pushes in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates more sun than most variegated types.

directions: Plant the white-and-gold striped clumps in well-drained soil where they can take sun better than other variegated lilyturf. Water during dry spells and shear old foliage in late winter. Lavender flower spikes rise above the bright foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; allow room for its large size.

directions: Give this robust lilyturf ample space and plant the broad, arching clumps in rich, well-drained soil. It forms large fountain-like mounds useful as a coarse groundcover or border anchor in shade to part sun. Lavender flower spikes appear among the tall blades in summer.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring in a warm, part-shaded spot.

directions: Plant the long dark green strap leaves in moist, fertile soil in part shade where the graceful clumps arch outward. Keep it watered and sheltered from hard frost since it is less cold-hardy than mondo grass. Nodding white flowers rise on flattened stems in summer, followed by violet-blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun or light shade.

directions: Set the tidy dark green clumps into well-drained soil for a dependable edging plant. Shear old leaves in late winter and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Violet flower spikes stand above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant tiny plugs closely in spring for a dense mat.

directions: Press the extremely dwarf, stiff tufts into loose soil just an inch or two apart for a mossy-looking carpet. This is one of the shortest mondo selections, ideal for miniature gardens, troughs, and between pavers. It needs no mowing and grows slowly into a dense green cushion.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade to sun.

directions: Plant the green grassy clumps in well-drained soil for a compact, uniform edging. This selection is named for its broad, tapering lavender flower spikes that resemble little conifers. Shear old foliage in late winter to keep the mound looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade for best new-leaf color.

directions: Set the clumps in fertile, moist soil in bright shade where the striking near-white new leaves show off before aging to green. Keep it out of hot afternoon sun to protect the pale foliage. Lavender flower spikes rise above the leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant the very dark, almost black tufts in humus-rich soil where they contrast with silver or chartreuse companions. Give part shade and steady moisture, and divide clumps to increase the display. Small pinkish flowers appear in summer, followed by shiny black berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade to sun.

directions: Plant the dark green clumps in well-drained soil for a reliable border or edging. This selection stands out for its soft pink flower spikes rather than the usual violet. Cut back old foliage in late winter before new blades emerge.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to keep the gold color bright.

directions: Plant the golden-yellow-leaved clumps in bright shade where the foliage color pops against darker plants. It keeps the best gold tone out of scorching sun, so give it morning light and afternoon shade. Lavender flower spikes appear above the bright leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade for best variegation.

directions: Set the gold-centered variegated clumps in moist, well-drained soil in bright shade. This selection is noted for wide leaves and unusual crested lavender flower spikes. Shear old foliage in late winter and divide when clumps grow crowded.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: Plant the broad-bladed dark green clumps in well-drained soil for a lush, full-textured edging. The wider leaves give a coarser look than standard lilyturf and fill space quickly. Violet flower spikes rise above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in part shade.

directions: Plant the white-striped, silvery clumps in part shade where the bright foliage lights up shaded ground. Like other creeping lilyturf it spreads by runners, so give it room or edge it in. Pale lavender flower spikes appear in summer among the luminous leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade to sun.

directions: Firm the dark green clumps into well-drained soil for edging beds and walkways. This selection blooms freely with abundant lilac-purple flower spikes held well above the foliage. Cut old leaves back in late winter to renew the planting each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade for heavy bloom.

directions: Plant the vigorous dark green clumps in well-drained soil where they can bloom heavily. This selection produces large, showy deep-purple flower spikes above robust foliage. Shear old leaves in late winter before new growth resumes.

planting.note: Plant small plugs closely in spring for a fine carpet.

directions: Tuck the compact dark green tufts a few inches apart into loose soil for a dense low mat. This dwarf selection knits together to make a fine turf substitute in shade and part sun. It rarely needs mowing and shows tiny lilac flowers hidden among the blades in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade to protect leaf margins.

directions: Plant the arching leaves edged in gold into moist, well-drained soil in bright shade. The broad, gently curving blades give a fountain-like form brightened by yellow margins. Lavender flower spikes appear above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade to sun.

directions: Set the dark green clumps into well-drained soil for a strong, upright edging. This older selection is grown for its deep violet, sometimes crested flower spikes held above dense foliage. Shear back old leaves in late winter to keep clumps tidy.

planting.note: Plant plugs closely in spring for a low, even mat.

directions: Plant the very short dark green tufts a few inches apart in loose, well-drained soil. This compact form spreads slowly into a tight, low carpet good for edging and small spaces. It needs little care beyond occasional grooming of winter-worn blades.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where a spreading cover is wanted.

directions: Plant this vigorous creeping selection in well-drained soil where its wider leaves and running habit can cover ground. It spreads by rhizomes to control erosion or fill large shaded areas, so contain it near tidy beds. Lavender flower spikes appear among the blades in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade for best variegation.

directions: Plant the white-striped grassy tufts in moist, well-drained soil in bright shade where the pale variegation stands out. Keep it out of harsh sun to prevent leaf burn and water in dry spells. It spreads slowly to form a luminous low groundcover.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade to sun.

directions: Plant the compact dark green clumps in well-drained soil for a neat, uniform edging. This selection blooms reliably with upright violet flower spikes above tidy foliage. Cut old leaves back in late winter so fresh growth emerges cleanly.

planting.note: Divide and replant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Set divisions into moist, cool, acidic woodland soil with the crown at grade. Keep steadily watered through summer, as the fronds wilt quickly if the soil dries. Upright, feathery fronds appear in spring and reach full height by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a moist, shaded spot.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in rich, moisture-retentive ground under trees or shade structures. Water regularly during dry spells and top-dress with leaf mold each fall. Arching fronds emerge with rosy new-growth tints that mature to green.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in cool shade.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, humus-rich, well-drained ground. Maintain even moisture and shade to keep the compact fronds fresh. This smaller lady fern spreads slowly into a low, tidy patch of finely cut foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade for the brightest silver color.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in moist, fertile soil enriched with compost. Give bright shade to intensify the ghostly silver-white coloring of this lady-fern cross. Upright fronds glow pale in spring and hold their luminous tone through the season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, moist border.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in cool, humus-rich, evenly moist ground. This upright hybrid blends the silver cast of painted fern with the vigor of lady fern, so give it light shade for best color. Silvery-green fronds on burgundy stems arch outward as the clump matures.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it can spread as a ground cover.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in moist, fertile shade and allow room for its running habit. Unlike clumping ferns, this selection travels on creeping rhizomes to knit together an open colony. Green fronds with a faint silver wash emerge along the spreading roots each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to highlight the silver foliage.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in cool, moist, compost-amended ground. Provide shade so the bright silver centers and dark red midribs stand out. The broad, shimmering fronds emerge in spring and form a low mounded clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: Plant the crown level with rich, damp soil and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Shade brings out the finely divided, pewter-toned fronds with their burgundy veining. Foliage unfurls in spring and layers into a lacy, low mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for the richest red midribs.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in fertile, consistently moist woodland soil. This selection shows especially deep red-purple midribs against silvery fronds when grown in shade. Keep it evenly watered and the color holds well into late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to develop the central red band.

directions: Set the crown flush with moist, humus-rich soil and shelter from strong sun. A broad reddish-purple flush runs down the center of each silvery frond. Provide steady moisture and shade to keep the contrast crisp through the season.

planting.note: Give this large form plenty of room in a moist, shaded bed.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in deep, rich, evenly moist ground and allow generous spacing. This is an oversized painted fern that spreads far wider than the typical selection. Silvery fronds with burgundy veins arch out to form a large, bold clump over a few seasons.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to show off the frond tips.

directions: Plant the crown level with moist, fertile soil in a shaded location. Each silvery frond ends in a forked, crested tip that gives the plant a frilled look. Keep the soil evenly damp and the ruffled fronds stay full through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, shaded border.

directions: Set the crown at grade in cool, humus-rich, consistently moist soil. This crested painted fern carries silver-and-burgundy fronds tipped with tasseled, forked ends. Grow it in shade with steady moisture for the fullest crests.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for the strongest coloring.

directions: Plant the crown flush with moist, compost-rich soil and keep it shaded. The fronds twist and curl as they mature, showing silver-gray surfaces and reddish veins. Maintain even moisture to keep the twisting foliage vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to bring out the red tones.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in rich, moist ground under shade. This selection is chosen for its pronounced red-purple midribs set against silvery fronds. Keep it evenly watered so the coloring stays vivid through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for classic silver-and-wine foliage.

directions: Set the crown level with humus-rich, evenly moist soil and shade it from hot sun. This is the classic painted fern, with metallic-silver fronds and burgundy stems and veins. Fronds unfurl in mid-spring and hold color into fall with steady moisture.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, shaded, sheltered bed.

directions: Set the crown level with rich, consistently moist soil in a shaded spot. This heirloom selection has crossed, latticed pinnae and crested frond tips that form a distinctive criss-cross pattern. Keep it evenly watered so the intricate fronds develop fully.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in damp, humus-rich ground and shelter from drying wind. Its unusual fronds carry rows of bead-like round lobes down a narrow midrib, resembling lace tatting. Keep the soil moist and the quirky fronds emerge fresh each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, shaded location.

directions: Set the crown level with rich, evenly moist soil in partial to full shade. This form has finely cut, crisped pinnae that give the fronds a delicate, feathery texture. Provide steady moisture and the lacy fronds stay lush through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a shaded bed.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil and keep it well watered. This miniature lady fern makes a compact rosette perfect for edging or small shade gardens. Neat, finely divided fronds unfurl in spring and stay tidy all season.
